title: Ceramic & Porcelain Tile Calculator 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
h1: How Many Boxes of Tiles Do You Need?
description: Tile calculator: ceramic & porcelain. Calculate boxes, m², adhesive & grout. Accounts for waste. All tile sizes. Free tool.
seoKeywords: how many tile boxes do i need
how much tile adhesive per square meter
intro: Calculate **how many boxes of ceramic or porcelain tile** you need for a flooring project. This calculator uses your **room dimensions** (length × width), your **tile size** (30×30, 45×45, **60×60 the most popular**, 80×80, 60×120 rectangular, or 100×100 large format) and a **waste percentage** (10% standard, 15–20% for diagonal or complex layouts). It outputs: **net m², m² to purchase, total boxes, estimated piece count, kg of tile adhesive** (brands like Klaukol or Weber) and **kg of grout** (for joints). Works with leading Argentine brands like **Cerro Negro, Ilva, San Lorenzo, Cortines, and Scop**, plus imported Italian porcelain. 2026 prices range from **$15,000/m²** in budget ceramic to **$140,000/m²** in large-format Italian porcelain.
keyTakeaway: **Boxes = round up ((room m² × (1 + waste%)) / m² per box)**. Standard **60×60 tiles** come in boxes of **1.44 m² (4 pieces)**. Use **10% waste for rectangular rooms**, **15–20% for diagonal or complex layouts**.
useCases: You're tiling a room and need to know how many boxes to order.
Planning a whole-house renovation and want to estimate material costs.
Comparing costs: large-format porcelain vs. standard ceramic pricing.
Replacing kitchen or bathroom tile and don't want to over-buy.
You're a tile installer calculating material for a customer quote.

example.title: Real example: 6×4 m living room with wood-look porcelain 60×60
steps: **Boxes needed**: 26.4 / 1.44 = 18.3 → round up to **19 boxes** (27.36 m² total).
**Adhesive**: 24 × 5 kg/m² = **120 kg = 6 bags of 20 kg** (Klaukol or Weber white).
**Material cost estimate**: 19 boxes × $80,000 = **$1,520,000** + adhesive $180,000 + grout $40,000 = **$1,740,000** materials only.
example.result: For your 24 m² living room, order **19 boxes of 60×60 porcelain** (27.36 m² purchased), **120 kg of adhesive**, and **9.6 kg of grout**. Add **$300,000–$500,000** for professional installation. Keep 2–3 spare pieces in case of breakage later.

explanation:
## Step-by-step calculation

### 1. Room area

```
m² = length × width
```

For irregular rooms (L-shaped or T-shaped), add the rectangular sections together. For curves or cutouts (columns, under-cabinet recesses), don't deduct unless very large.

### 2. Area with waste

```
m²_to_buy = m² × (1 + waste%)
```

**Recommended waste by layout type**:

| Layout Type | Waste % |
|---|---|
| **Straight (parallel to walls)** | **10%** |
| Diagonal corners or irregular corners | 15% |
| 45° diagonal layout | 15–20% |
| Large-format (80×80+) with many cuts | 15–20% |
| Herringbone or chevron | 20–25% |
| Mosaic or decorative patterns | 20–30% |

### 3. Total boxes needed

```
boxes = round_up(m²_to_buy / m²_per_box)
```

Always round up—you can't buy a partial box.

## m² per box by tile size (standard)

| Tile Size (cm) | m² per box | Pieces per box | Approx. kg per box |
|---|---|---|---|
| 15×15 | 1.35 | 60 | 22 |
| 20×20 | 1.60 | 40 | 26 |
| 30×30 | 1.62 | 18 | 28 |
| 33×33 | 1.63 | 15 | 28 |
| 45×45 | 1.62 | 8 | 30 |
| **60×60** | **1.44** | **4** | 32 |
| 80×80 | 1.28 | 2 | 34 |
| 60×120 | 1.44 | 2 | 35 |
| 100×100 | 2.00 | 2 | 48 |
| 15×90 (wood-look) | 1.22 | 9 | 26 |
| 20×120 (wood-look) | 1.44 | 6 | 32 |

> Brands vary ±5%. Check the exact m² on your box before buying.

## Tile adhesive (thin-set mortar)

### How much adhesive per m²

| Tile Type | Adhesive per m² |
|---|---|
| Small ceramic (30×30) | 3–4 kg/m² |
| Ceramic 45×45 | 4 kg/m² |
| Ceramic 60×60 | **4–5 kg/m²** |
| Porcelain 80+ | 5–7 kg/m² (full bed) |
| Extra-large 100×100+ | 7–8 kg/m² |
| Outdoor / heavy traffic | 7–8 kg/m² |

### Common brands and bag sizes

| Brand | Type | Typical Bag |
|---|---|---|
| **Klaukol** | Standard, premium, flexible | 25–30 kg |
| **Weber** (Saint-Gobain) | Colla Fix, ProFlex | 20–25 kg |
| **Iggam** | Diamond, Gold | 25–30 kg |
| **Sika** | Sikaceram | 20 kg |
| **Parex** | Universal adhesive | 25 kg |

Adhesives are rated by standard (C1T, C2TE, C2TE S1) based on flexibility and strength. For **porcelain or outdoor use**, pick **C2TE minimum**.

## Grout (for joints)

### How much grout needed

| Joint Width | Grout per m² |
|---|---|
| 1–2 mm | 0.2–0.3 kg/m² |
| 2–3 mm (standard) | **0.3–0.5 kg/m²** |
| 3–5 mm | 0.5–0.8 kg/m² |
| 5+ mm (rustic) | 0.8–1.2 kg/m² |

### Types and colors

- **Cement-based**: affordable, basic colors (white, gray, beige).
- **Epoxy**: waterproof, anti-mold, for wet areas. 3–5× more expensive.
- **Flexible**: for radiant floors or high-movement areas.
- **Tintable**: white + color pigment to match.

Brands: **Klaukol Grout, Weber Joint, Sikaceram Grout**.

## Tile types

### Glazed ceramic

- Budget-friendly ($15,000–$40,000/m²).
- Interior, low-traffic areas (bedrooms, bathrooms).
- High water absorption (5–15%).
- Scratch resistance: low–medium (PEI 3–4).

### Porcelain tile

- Mid to premium ($30,000–$140,000/m²).
- Durable everywhere including outdoor.
- Very low absorption (<0.5%).
- Scratch resistance: high (PEI 4–5).

### Large-format (80×80, 60×120, 100×100)

- Modern, fewer grout lines.
- Requires special adhesive (full bed) and two-person installation.
- **Lay at English bond** (staggered, max 33% overlap) because large tiles have slight natural curvature.
- Price: 40–80% premium over 60×60.

### Wood-look porcelain

- Rectangular format (15×90, 20×120, 22×120).
- Mimics wood without upkeep.
- Trending 2025–2026.
- Price similar to standard porcelain.

## Waste by installation pattern

| Pattern | Waste % |
|---|---|
| **Straight (parallel to walls)** | **10%** |
| **45° diagonal** | 15–20% |
| **Herringbone / chevron** | 20–25% |
| **Irregular mosaic** | 15–20% |
| **Offset (33% stagger)** | 10–12% |
| **Multi-room continuous** | 10% |

## 2026 price guide (Argentina)

| Product | Price |
|---|---|
| Budget ceramic 45×45 | $15,000–$25,000/m² |
| Mid-range ceramic 60×60 | $30,000–$50,000/m² |
| Wood-look porcelain | $50,000–$90,000/m² |
| Large-format porcelain | $80,000–$140,000/m² |
| Imported Italian porcelain | $120,000–$300,000/m² |
| Klaukol standard adhesive (30 kg) | $25,000–$35,000 |
| Klaukol flexible premium (30 kg) | $35,000–$55,000 |
| Cement grout (5 kg) | $15,000–$25,000 |
| Epoxy grout (5 kg) | $50,000–$90,000 |

### Installation labor

Add **$15,000–$30,000/m²** for professional installation:
- **Standard ceramic**: $15,000–$20,000/m².
- **Porcelain standard**: $20,000–$25,000/m².
- **Large-format or diagonal**: $25,000–$30,000/m².
- **Wood-look or herringbone**: $25,000–$35,000/m².

## Pro tips

1. **Check shade and caliber** on the box before buying. Different batches vary.
2. **Use spacers** (2–3 mm for porcelain, 1.5 mm for small tile, 5 mm for rustic).
3. **Always grout**—never lay tiles touching. Thermal expansion will crack them.
4. **Radiant heat**: use flexible adhesive and low-expansion tiles (porcelain).
5. **Cutting**: manual cutter for 60×60 and smaller, wet saw with diamond blade for 80+.
6. **Large-format**: use plastic leveling wedges to avoid stepped surfaces.
7. **Keep spares**: save 2–3 pieces per pattern for future repairs. Batches don't repeat.
8. **Pre-wet or not**: porcelain tiles don't need pre-wetting. Porous ceramic should soak 5 minutes.

## Installation order

1. **Level subfloor** with 1% slope to drain (for bathrooms/outdoor).
2. **Layout**: mark center point, start from the most visible spot. First row should use whole tiles.
3. **Adhesive**: spread with proper notched trowel. 10 mm trowel for 60×60.
4. **Tile**: tap into place with rubber mallet.
5. **Spacers**: maintain even joints.
6. **Grout**: after 24–48 hours when adhesive is fully cured.
7. **Cleanup**: damp cloth while grout is wet, dry sponge afterward.

- [Paint calculator](/paint-calculator-by-room-square-meters) — for the same room's walls and ceiling.
- [Construction cost per m²](/construction-cost-calculator-square-meter-argentina) — full project budget.

faq.q: How do I calculate how many tile boxes I need?
faq.a: Multiply room length × width to get m². Add waste (10% for straight layouts, 15–20% for diagonal). Divide total m² by m² per box. Round up to the nearest whole box. Example: 20 m² + 10% waste = 22 m². If each box is 1.44 m², you need 22 ÷ 1.44 = 15.3 → **16 boxes**.

faq.q: What waste percentage should I add?
faq.a: Add **10%** for rectangular rooms with straight installation. Add **15–20%** if you're tiling diagonally (45°), or if you have irregular corners and many cuts. Add up to **25%** for herringbone or mosaic patterns. Always safer to over-buy slightly—you can't get the exact same batch months later.

faq.q: How much tile adhesive do I need per square meter?
faq.a: For **60×60 ceramic or porcelain**: **4–5 kg/m²**. For **small tiles (30×30)**: 3–4 kg/m². For **large-format (80×80+)**: 5–8 kg/m² using a full bed technique. One standard 25–30 kg bag covers roughly 5–7 m² of 60×60 tile.

faq.q: What's the difference between ceramic and porcelain tile?
faq.a: **Ceramic**: glazed clay, affordable ($15–50k/m²), absorbs water (5–15%), scratches easier (PEI 3–4), good for low-traffic indoor areas. **Porcelain**: fired at higher temp, non-porous (<0.5% absorption), stain-resistant, resists scratches (PEI 4–5), costs more ($30–140k/m²) but lasts 3–5× longer. Porcelain is best for kitchens, bathrooms, and outdoor spaces.

faq.q: How much grout (joint filler) do I need?
faq.a: For **2–3 mm joints** (standard): **0.3–0.5 kg/m²**. A 5 kg bag covers roughly 10–15 m² of floor. Use **cement-based grout** for regular interiors, **epoxy grout** for high-moisture areas (bathrooms, kitchens). Epoxy is pricier but resists stains and mold better.

faq.q: Should I use ceramic or porcelain for my bathroom?
faq.a: **Porcelain is better for bathrooms**. It's nearly waterproof (<0.5% absorption), resists mold, and won't stain easily. Choose 60×60 or larger sizes to minimize grout lines (less mold hiding spots). Use epoxy grout for joints if possible. Ceramic works but absorbs water and may need more maintenance.

faq.q: Can I install new tile over old tile?
faq.a: Yes, if the old tile is well-adhered and smooth. Steps: 1) Deep clean with degreaser. 2) Sand or apply bonding agent to remove shine. 3) Use **flexible C2TE adhesive**. The floor height increases ~10–12 mm, which may catch door bottoms (you'll need to trim them). For quick renovations, it saves demolition time and cost.

faq.q: How much does tile installation labor cost?
faq.a: In 2026 (Argentina): **$15,000–$20,000/m²** for standard ceramic, **$20,000–$25,000/m²** for porcelain, **$25,000–$30,000/m²** for large-format or diagonal, **$25,000–$35,000/m²** for herringbone/wood-look. For a 20 m² room at medium complexity (porcelain): expect $400,000–$500,000 in labor alone. Always get 3 quotes.

faq.q: What size tile should I choose for different rooms?
faq.a: **Bedrooms**: any size (small 30×30 to large 80×80). **Kitchens**: 60×60 or larger (fewer grout lines to clean). **Bathrooms**: 60×60 or larger, preferably porcelain for water resistance. **Wood-look (wood-grain look)**: rectangular 20×120 is trending. **Outdoor**: large-format porcelain (80+) for modern look. **High-traffic**: porcelain, any size (durability over looks).

faq.q: What's the difference between cement-based and epoxy grout?
faq.a: **Cement-based**: cheaper ($15–25k/5 kg), common for interiors, comes in basic colors. Stains more easily, needs sealing in kitchens. **Epoxy**: waterproof, stain-proof, mold-resistant ($50–90k/5 kg), ideal for bathrooms, kitchens, pools. Harder to apply (more working time). Lasts 2–3× longer in wet areas, worth the extra cost.
